tweets.text:
It's not a coincidence Jamie Dimon, Elon, Jensen, and Barry Diller all share the same management philosophy: getting the truth from the source

Dara on what he learned from Barry Diller in his 20+ years working with him:

"I was an analyst at Allen & Company working on the LBO model for Paramount. 

Barry didn't want to talk to the MD or the VP or the associate. 

He'd say, 'Who built the model? I'm going to talk to that guy.' 

He wanted to hear straight from the source.

It's the filtering that gets the edge out of the situation, and it's often the edge that gives you an edge. It's not the average."

@GavinSBaker in our conversation from 2024 explained how Elon, Jensen, and Jamie work:

"Wherever in the company the problem is, that is who Jensen and Elon go to work with — the subject matter expert, whether they're 23 or 50, there's no hierarchy. 

When JPMorgan was buying Bear Stearns, the best modeler in the company was 24 years old. 

They set up a desk for him side-by-side with Jamie. Jamie would say, "Change that, change this." 

He didn't ask for that guy's boss. He said, 'This guy is the best, I want to work with him.'"

trading is so much more about emotional regulation than anything or at least it's one of the biggest parts of it. and we all fall victim to it at times as well. I think you are in a good spot if a good or bad trade doesn't affect the way you treat others or really affect your personal life so much

a few other random thoughts, i do keep a ton of cash on hand at all time. likely more than i should which sometimes leaves me under-allocated but when things shit the bed it also gives me good opportunities to make a lot and return the cash. It is my defense, imagine playing soccer without a goalie, or hockey without defenseman. trading is a two way game. offense and defense. both need to be good. too many people are offense only and high stakes. but i also have a pretty complex portfolio setup by design which has it's pros and cons

you just have to accept that you are going to lose sometimes. its about being a good loser though, and knowing when you are wrong on something quickly

The Central Bank of Iran reported an annual inflation rate of more than 50.6% in April. According to the same report, monthly inflation spiked to 67%.
Prices of some goods and services rose by up to 100% during the same period, vastly outpacing stagnant wage growth.
To ease the acute economic pressure, Iran is pushing for the release of roughly $24 billion in assets frozen in foreign banks.
Tehran hopes access to those funds could help stabilize the currency market, lower inflationary expectations and reduce the cost of importing basic goods and raw materials. Iranian officials have said “meaningful negotiations will not begin without the release of these assets.”

thread_tweets.text:
2. Acquisition is the bottleneck.

No, it's not.
A lot of Web3 consumer products do not have an acquisition problem.
They have an activation problem.

The campaign gets users to register.
The product fails to make them use it twice.
That is why “we have 500K users” often means very little without active usage data.

thread_tweets.text:
4. Referral programs protect safe UAC.

They don't. It's ez to game 90% of Web3 ref programs.

Most referral programs reward the wrong behavior.
They pay for signups.
But signups are cheap to fake. The better model is to reward activation, spend, repeat usage, or another behavior tied to actual product value.

KAST’s spend-linked referral design is a good example.

thread_tweets.text:
5. Transparency is optional

A huge illusion.
Public metrics are underrated as a GTM channel.
In Web3, traction updates are not only investor comms.
They become social proof.

In neobanks context: Spend volume, active cards, cashback paid, transaction growth, user milestones – all of this gives the market something to repeat.

Silence creates friction.

Noble Plains #Uranium @NOBLuranium $NOBL $NOBL.V just filed its NI 43-101 technical report for Duck Creek, and the story looks even better when you dig into the details.

✅ Maiden resource: 6.36M lbs U3O8 (5.32M lbs Indicated + 1.04M lbs Inferred)

✅ Resource exceeded its own Exploration Target by 30% on grade and 40% on GT

✅ Only ~2.5 miles drilled, while another ~2.75 miles of mineralized trend remains largely untested

✅ Additional Exploration Target already identified outside the current resource area

✅ Potential upside from the deeper Fort Union Formation not included in the resource

Most junior uranium companies would be thrilled to have a 6+ Mlbs resource. Noble Plains has that resource on roughly half the trend, with significant expansion potential still ahead.

What stands out is that the maiden resource didn't merely meet expectations — it outperformed the company's own target range. That's often a sign the geological model may be conservative.

Location matters too. Duck Creek sits in Wyoming's Powder River Basin, surrounded by projects owned by Cameco and UEC. In-situ recovery (ISR) deposits in established U.S. uranium districts continue to attract premium valuations as utilities and governments prioritize domestic supply.

2026 drilling now becomes the key catalyst. If management can convert the remaining Exploration Target into resources and demonstrate continuity along strike, today's 6.36M lbs could end up being just the beginning.

The market is valuing NOBL as a small explorer. The geology is increasingly suggesting something larger.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Noble Plains Uranium. This is not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

thread_tweets.text:
In 1999, Ray Kurzweil predicted human-level AI by 2029. Stanford's top AI experts said 100 years. Three years from the deadline, he says two things are still missing: AI that understands physics, and a robot that knows the difference between what goes in the fridge and what goes in the trash.

-- LLMs went from unremarkable to world-changing in 12 months. AI now outperforms human doctors by 50% in diagnosis.

 -- LLMs have only been truly effective for six months, according to Ray. A year ago, "not really all that impressive."

 -- The Genome Project was 1% done at the halfway mark. One doubling later — done. We're at that same moment with AGI. 

-- Software alone has improved roughly a millionfold over 70 years. Total compute gain = hardware (75,000 trillion fold) × software (million fold).

tweets.text:
$1.7B+ liquidated in 24 hours and around 290K traders wiped out.

That's what happens when everyone gets a little too comfortable with leverage.

Tthe liquidation number isn't the one I keep coming back to. Only ~24% of the top 100 coins are above their 50D trend, which tells me the market isn't healthy under the surface.

A few names might bounce, but most of the board is still sitting below trend.

Is this full macro panic? I don't think so. Traditional markets are holding up fine, so the pressure feels crypto-specific to me.

Leverage got way too crowded, and high beta is always the first thing people dump when they want to de-risk.

ETF demand cooling off into all of this didn't help either... the flows just aren't strong enough right now to say institutions are stepping in to absorb the dip.

So I'm playing it defensively for now. Until breadth picks back up and the ETF bid returns, I don't see a reason to get aggressive here.

It's a good time to be building watchlists though. The names you actually want are getting cheaper, and most people are too busy panicking to notice.

Tthe liquidation number isn't the one I keep coming back to. Only ~24% of the top 100 coins are above their 50D trend, which tells me the market isn't healthy under the surface.

A few names might bounce, but most of the board is still sitting below trend.

Is this full macro panic? I don't think so. Traditional markets are holding up fine, so the pressure feels crypto-specific to me.

Leverage got way too crowded, and high beta is always the first thing people dump when they want to de-risk.

ETF demand cooling off into all of this didn't help either... the flows just aren't strong enough right now to say institutions are stepping in to absorb the dip.

So I'm playing it defensively for now. Until breadth picks back up and the ETF bid returns, I don't see a reason to get aggressive here.

It's a good time to be building watchlists though. The names you actually want are getting cheaper, and most people are too busy panicking to notice.

For anyone with FOMO or thinking they should have known better: Don’t feel bad. 

No one has nailed the market this year, NO ONE. 

Not @LukeGromen. Not @jvisserlabs. And certainly not the permabears like @DowdEdward. 

They have all gotten it wrong in key aspects. 

AS HAVE I.

This has been a tough market to call right.

For example, Luke has basically been calling to not get “over your skies” on equities in general since the tariff debacle last year when the S&P was around 5,000. Up 50% since then. 

Jordi was advocating to sell semis like Micron around $600-700 and move into BTC and silver a month or so ago. He also said not to hope for new highs this year in the major indexes. Incorrect to be modest. 

Ed has been bearish forever.

As for me, I expected a BTC rebound. I nailed the bottom on equites and called for big buys on April 1, especially in semis, but way too early I said time to book those gains and rotate into gold, XLB, EWZ, and more traditional cyclical calls. 

BTW, I think that call I made early is probably starting to be right now.

At the end of day, I hope all have made money. I have. I’m sure Luke has with Gold as has Jordi with his AI plays and thematic baskets. But as for Ed, sorry bro. Give up the bearishness. It's really getting old. 

S&P will hit 10,000 by end of next year.
